name: Madrid: Green Cycling Ring
region_intro: Road cycling routes around Area Metropolitana De Madrid traverse a diverse landscape, ranging from the relatively flat urban and suburban areas to the challenging mountainous terrain of the Sierra de Guadarrama. The region features rugged peaks, deep valleys, and expansive forests, offering varied elevation profiles. Additionally, tranquil routes can be found around several reservoirs and through river valleys, providing options away from urban traffic. Best road cycling routes around Area Metropolitana De Madrid The most popular road cycling route is Madrid: Green Cycling Ring, a 39.6 miles (63.7 km) trail that takes 3 hours 15 minutes to complete. This moderate route encircles the city, connecting various green spaces. Another top favourite among local road cyclists is Villanueva de la Cañada, Zarzalejo, Navalagamella – circular route, a difficult 41.4 miles (66.6 km) path. This route offers significant elevation gain through the western part of the region. Local road cyclists also love the Mejorada del Campo, Velilla de San Antonio, San Martín de la Vega – circular route, a 44.0 miles (70.8 km) trail leading through the eastern plains and river valleys, often completed in about 3 hours 8 minutes. Road cycling around Area Metropolitana De Madrid is defined by the Sierra de Guadarrama mountains, urban green spaces, and river valleys. The network offers options for different ability levels, from leisurely rides to demanding climbs. The routes in Area Metropolitana De Madrid are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 1,000 reviews. More than 58,000 road cyclists have used komoot to explore Area Metropolitana De Madrid's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many road cycling routes are available in Area Metropolitana De Madrid?
A: There are over 540 road cycling routes in the Area Metropolitana De Madrid. These routes cater to various skill levels, from easy rides to challenging climbs, and are highly rated by the komoot community.

Q: What is the best season for road cycling in Area Metropolitana De Madrid?
A: The Mediterranean climate in the Area Metropolitana De Madrid offers favorable conditions for road cycling for most of the year. Spring and autumn are particularly pleasant, with fewer rainy days and comfortable temperatures, making it ideal for exploring the region's diverse landscapes.

Q: Are there road cycling routes suitable for beginners or families?
A: Yes, the region offers numerous options for less experienced cyclists and families. You can find over 100 easy routes, many of which traverse urban green spaces or tranquil areas around reservoirs. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e980659190/tres-cantos-complete" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Tres Cantos complete&lt;/a&gt; route is an easy option, covering about 34 km with minimal elevation gain.

Q: Are there challenging road cycling routes for experienced cyclists?
A: Absolutely. The Sierra de Guadarrama mountains, located northwest of Madrid, provide demanding climbs and significant elevation gains. Routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e977181990/villanueva-de-la-canada-zarzalejo-navalagamella-circular-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Villanueva de la Cañada, Zarzalejo, Navalagamella – circular route&lt;/a&gt; offer over 1,000 meters of elevation gain, while the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/14947640" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto del Cerro de San Pedro – Puerto de Canencia climb loop from Colmenar Viejo&lt;/a&gt; features over 2,100 meters of ascent.

Q: Are there circular road cycling routes in the Area Metropolitana De Madrid?
A: Yes, many routes in the region are designed as circular loops, allowing you to start and end in the same location. Popular examples include the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e977036167/madrid-green-cycling-ring" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Madrid: Green Cycling Ring&lt;/a&gt;, which encircles the city, and the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e977085239/mejorada-del-campo-velilla-de-san-antonio-san-martin-de-la-vega-circular-route" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Mejorada del Campo, Velilla de San Antonio, San Martín de la Vega – circular route&lt;/a&gt;, exploring the eastern plains.

Q: What natural features can I explore on road cycling routes in Area Metropolitana De Madrid?
A: Road cycling routes in the Area Metropolitana De Madrid offer access to diverse natural features. You can cycle through the rugged peaks and valleys of the Sierra de Guadarrama, explore the tranquil areas around reservoirs like Embalse del Atazar, or enjoy the scenic beauty of the Lozoya Valley. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2497364" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Alto del Cerro de San Pedro&lt;/a&gt; is a notable summit you might encounter.

Q: Are there any historical or cultural landmarks accessible by road bike?
A: Yes, many routes pass through or near towns with significant historical and cultural value. El Escorial, with its UNESCO World Heritage monastery, and Aranjuez, another UNESCO site known for its royal palace and gardens, are accessible by road bike. Within Madrid, you can cycle through areas near &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4925113" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Monument to the Sacred Heart of Jesus of Cerro de los Ángeles&lt;/a&gt; or enjoy views of the city skyline from &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1093455" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Parque Lineal del Manzanares&lt;/a&gt;.

Q: Where can I find parking for road cycling routes in Area Metropolitana De Madrid?
A: Many towns and starting points for routes in the Area Metropolitana De Madrid offer public parking facilities. For routes venturing into the Sierra de Guadarrama, towns like Soto del Real, Miraflores de la Sierra, or El Escorial often have designated parking areas. Within Madrid, parks like Casa de Campo also provide parking options.

Q: Can I access road cycling routes using public transport with my bike?
A: Yes, public transport options, particularly regional trains (Cercanías), allow bikes on board, making it convenient to reach starting points for routes outside the city center. This is especially useful for accessing routes in the Sierra de Guadarrama or other natural parks, allowing you to combine cycling with public transport for a broader exploration of the region.

Q: Are there places to stop for refreshments along the routes?
A: Many road cycling routes in the Area Metropolitana De Madrid pass through or near villages and towns where you can find cafes, restaurants, and shops for refreshments. Even on more rural routes, it's common to find establishments catering to cyclists, especially in popular cycling areas like the Lozoya Valley or towns surrounding the Sierra de Guadarrama.

Q: What do other road cyclists enjoy most about the routes in Area Metropolitana De Madrid?
A: The road cycling routes in Area Metropolitana De Madrid are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.5 stars from over 1,000 reviews. Cyclists often praise the varied terrain, from the challenging mountain passes to the tranquil reservoir routes, and the accessibility of natural areas from the city. The well-paved roads and scenic beauty are frequently highlighted as key attractions.

Q: Are there less crowded road cycling trails in the Area Metropolitana De Madrid?
A: While popular routes can see more traffic, you can find less crowded options, especially around the various reservoirs like Embalse del Atazar or Pantano de Puentes Viejas. These areas often offer tranquil rides away from the urban bustle. Exploring routes during off-peak hours or weekdays can also provide a more serene experience.

Q: Are there road cycling routes that offer views of the Madrid skyline?
A: Yes, some routes and viewpoints provide excellent vistas of the Madrid skyline. For instance, cycling through parts of Casa de Campo or from specific points in the Parque Lineal del Manzanares, such as the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/1093455" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of the Madrid skyline from Parque Lineal del Manzanares&lt;/a&gt;, can offer panoramic views of the city.
tips: It is a good refreshment site for cyclists from the Capital who use the Casa de Campo. Quiet place for a relaxed chat.
Lovely stretch of road along the river. Cool, shady and with many trees.

The San Fernando bridge is located in the northwestern part of the municipal term of Madrid. It stands on the Manzanares River,1 in an area near the Monte de El Pardo and the Hippodrome de la Zarzuela, next to the A-6 and the Puerta de Hierro junction.

Philip V ordered a road to be built from the capital to the Royal Site of El Pardo, of which this bridge would form a part. It was built in 1749, during the reign of Fernando VI. Its design was entrusted to the architect Jaime Bort y Meliá. The statues were made by the sculptor Vicente Bort, brother of the architect.

The Cívitas Metropolitano is the home of Atlético de Madrid. It opened in 2017 on the site of the former athletics stadium known as "La Peineta." Located in Madrid, it has a capacity for over 70,000 fans and is known for its modern architecture and distinctive roof.
The rise parallel to Avenida de Miguel Hernandez is ideal for practicing series. In the park, next to the green ring, there is a long stretch of hills ideal for mountain biking.
It is a very large park and has many beautiful corners to appreciate. There are bike paths, a lake where you can go canoeing and other activities, and a botanical garden area.
